summary:
The Lengyel culture was a Late Neolithic and early Copper Age culture of central Europe, distributed across Hungary, Austria, Moravia, Bohemia, and parts of Poland between approximately 5000 and 4000 BCE. It developed from the earlier [[event:1551|Linear Pottery culture]] tradition and represents one of the most geographically extensive and culturally rich Neolithic cultures of the Danube basin. The Lengyel culture is particularly notable for its characteristic polished and painted pottery, its large enclosed settlements (Kreisgrabenanlagen or rondels), and its evidence of early copper use.

Lengyel communities constructed large circular or oval enclosures consisting of ditches and palisades, sometimes with elaborate entrance arrangements. These rondels — found at dozens of sites across the Lengyel cultural zone — may have served as ceremonial gathering spaces, astronomical observatories, or bounded areas for communal ritual. Their construction required substantial organized labor and implies a degree of social complexity capable of coordinating community-wide projects. The Lengyel culture overlapped chronologically and geographically with the emergence of Copper Age metallurgy in southeastern Europe, and Lengyel communities participated in the exchange of copper objects that characterized the Chalcolithic Balkan networks.

The Lengyel cultural tradition contributed to the broader cultural landscape of Neolithic central Europe and is ancestral to several later regional cultures. It represents an important phase in the development of European social complexity before the dramatic demographic transformations of the late Neolithic and early Bronze Age associated with steppe migrations.
